facts.fact: Quel'Thalas's ward network is experienced bodily by trained elves as a hum or pressure rather than as abstract theory alone.
The ward-stones can register unauthorized crossings and react strangely without being visibly blasted or shattered.
Jaina was capable of layering diagnostic spellwork over the Thalassian runes while the disturbance was active.
A magical anomaly can be described in practical terms by non-magister personnel: the stones sounded wrong, the hum shifted, and then settled after intervention stopped.
Thalassian wards can actively damp a mage's perception, meaning release from the ward-circle returns magical input as a bodily surge rather than as a simple increase in power.
The chapter's necromantic contamination rides more than ley lines: it can inhabit soil, runoff, root systems, and living tissue while still using the existing magical lattice as a carrier.
Necromantic pressure in living creatures can keep broken bodies moving past mortal injury, changing kill logic from ordinary center-mass shots to destruction of eye or skull.
By chapter 3 the contamination is proven to saturate entire built environments: house interiors, cellar stone, troughs, road cuts, and the granary all carry it at once.
A magically loud site is not automatically the source; Jaina distinguishes the loudest layer from the deepest cause in the Millhaven granary.
By chapter 4 Jaina can stabilize some infected patients temporarily with ward-work, but only as delay; once ingestion continues, the magic cannot solve the underlying mechanism.
The plague's living-body progression is gut-first and cumulative, with repeated ingestion settling the contamination deeper rather than functioning like a single poisoned swallow.

The treated substance exists in concentrated liquid form before mixing and is crafted well enough to survive road, storehouse, oven, and flesh, marking it as skilled applied necromancy beyond Jaina's approved Dalaran training.
Chapter 5 establishes that deliberate necromantic residue can be hidden inside a staged ordinary death, making magical evidence something a skilled enemy can bury rather than display.
Jaina can recover useful magical truth from mixed forensic scenes by pairing spellwork with ash, scoring, route marks, and other physical evidence instead of reading magic in isolation.
By chapter 6 Jaina can articulate the plague as layered necromantic design: grain as shell, repetition as conditioning, and the body's own memory as part of what survives death.
Active corruption at city scale can register in bodies and mounts before the source is directly entered, turning magical pressure into a pre-visual approach sense.

Chapter 7 proves the grain receives a preserving necromantic shell during repack in Andorhal, with residue strongest in dust on high beams and boards, while the host body still completes the deeper transformation later.
Chapter 8 adds a new plague-side magical capability: precise remote witness silencing that feels like a needle through distance rather than a broad wave of corruption.
The lower field necromancers in Andorhal function as obedience or command-signal acolytes, confirming a split between carrier work, control work, and higher design.
Jaina can repurpose her magical attention from diagnosis to live urban threat reading, tracking hostile pressure through walls and across roofs quickly enough to guide the team at run speed.
The body is part of the plague's lock rather than only a carrier, and the preserving work survives storage and baking before the final trigger arrives.
Ritual sites can hide alarm lines, layered anti-intrusion wards, and scout-spacing traps inside physically persuasive terrain.
Chapter 10 confirms Scholomance's work grows directly out of Kirin Tor notation and ward logic rather than rejecting that lineage.
The outer ward at Darrowmere flags magical entrants during the lake crossing, making approach itself part of the institution's surveillance system.
Resonance is the key concept organizing the missing animation trigger: conditioned tissue is taught to answer patterned amplification after death rather than merely being forced from outside.
Chapter 12 completes the plague mechanism: the grain is only a preserving carrier shell, repeated ingestion teaches the host body, and death releases an answer already conditioned into tissue.
Low-fidelity village undead and high-fidelity preserved selfhood belong to the same magical continuum; the difference is degree, cost, and structure rather than separate schools of magic.
Necromancy in this story is explicitly framed as a continuous extension of accepted arcane theory, which is why Dalaran's ban had to be moral rather than mathematical.
Deep ward architecture in Scholomance can relay one intruder's descent as a sequence of answered thresholds all the way to the sanctum below.
Chapter 13 confirms the outer Darrowmere ward can be muted by hugging the east wall rather than taking the proper landing.
The plague mechanism can now be stated in field form: grain carries the outer shell, the body learns the deeper answer through exposure, and death lets it wake.
Scholomance ward systems begin slipping once Kel'Thuzad is gone from the center.
The Darrowmere outer ward feels entrants more than it cleanly blocks them, and its pressure weakens along the east wall enough to support covert approach.
Jaina can turn existing room geometry plus frost into containment fast enough to cage a stronger necromancer for an allied shot.
After Kel'Thuzad falls, the sanctum and surrounding wards keep cycling for a time and then begin to slip, proving the system is not identical with the man but was still stabilized by his presence.
Chapter 14 operationalizes the plague mechanism: the body-learning theory can now be translated into feed-point intervention logic aimed at granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards.
Any immediate magical answer is narrow rather than curative; cold or fire might destroy contaminated stock before ingestion, but neither approach reverses what has already settled into living tissue.
Kel'Thuzad's expected lich return is now treated as field intelligence rather than as a private sanctum revelation.
Kel’Thuzad’s death does not stop the dead from rising in the field, proving the plague can sustain active recurrence without the architect physically present at each corpse.
Holy fire and the Light are effective against rising dead in the immediate moment, but function as containment and interruption rather than true cure of the broader plague system.
The completed body-learning mechanism can now be converted into source-side intervention logic: destroy contaminated stock before it becomes fully embodied.
Chapter 16 confirms the preserving shell on contaminated stock can be broken at warehouse level, destroying the load before it reaches the body but not undoing what has already been eaten.
The magical answer to the plague is now sharply bounded: source-side destruction works locally, but there is no clean spell solution once bread, kitchens, and bodies are already inside the cycle.
Chapter 18 revises the field model again: grain is still the preserving shell, but contaminated bodies appear to hold a deeper pattern that can answer something farther away.
The plague mechanism now plausibly includes remote activation or relay logic, meaning Kel'Thuzad's death removed a node without collapsing the full system.
Chapter 19 confirms Jaina can track the deeper pattern by directional resonance strongly enough to justify coastward pursuit even without new local physical samples.
Chapter 20 confirms Jaina's resonance is bidirectional: probing toward the source announces her strongly enough to draw active resistance back across the link.
Supernatural headwinds can function as hostile magical opposition during travel, turning a sea crossing itself into contested ground.
By chapter20 the bread-taste has become baseline rather than intermittent warning, implying sustained proximity or lock to the deeper source.
Chapter21 confirms Frostmourne is magically kin to the plague system: not an unrelated cursed relic, but a sister artifact made by the same hostile hand.
Frostmourne projects a field that catches and throws direct spell force sideways rather than merely resisting it, giving the blade an active anti-intervention geometry.
The field around Frostmourne is not smooth; Jaina detects a repeatable hitch or seam inside its deflection pattern even under combat pressure.
Arthas after claiming Frostmourne appears to obey altered magical rules, surviving direct force and ordinary lethal standards in ways his living body previously would not have.
Chapter22 converts the seam in Frostmourne’s field into a three-school anti-blade sequence: frost to force the turn, arcane to hold the seam, and fire through the exposed gap.
The anti-Frostmourne framework only functions if another person can create three to five still seconds at sword range, making bodily choreography part of the spell rather than external support.

Jaina’s widened resonance channel cannot simply be turned off after Northrend; the return chapter shows it persisting as tremor, nosebleed, collapse risk, and the constant bread-warm taint even far from the original cave.
Old peacetime Kirin Tor and palace warding can still be softened at a service lock by coordinated magi, but that access does not extend to clean authority over the room beyond.
Chapter23 confirms the anti-Frostmourne sequence works in live combat: frost can force the turn, arcane pressure can hold the seam open, and fire can drive through the exposed breach.
Damaging Frostmourne’s bond to Arthas is not the same as neutralizing Frostmourne itself; the blade remains active after Arthas dies.

Frostmourne can release violent soul-echo pressure at the moment its bond is ruptured, suggesting the weapon stores trapped dead or memory residue that is not automatically freed by the wielder’s death.
In chapter24 Frostmourne remains magically active in vault custody, projecting cold and intent through palace stone strongly enough for Jaina to feel it from the cells below.
Jaina's chapter24 estimate reduces the containment window to days, implying the damaged blade is already searching for a new mind to answer it.
Two dozen elven mages can independently sense the blade's wrongness on arrival, proving Frostmourne's field is legible beyond Jaina's unique attunement.
